nodes.label: Vestibule
nodes.rationale: A local, kernel-isolated code-execution sandbox for AI agents shipped as an MCP server; exists to refuse the three costs of hosted sandboxes (E2B/Daytona): per-usage billing, a network round-trip in the agent loop, and shipping generated code plus data to third-party infrastructure.

nodes.label: Local + Free + No-Cloud value proposition
nodes.rationale: Local: execution is a process spawn not an HTTP call, works offline. Free: MIT, no account, no metering. No cloud: code and data never leave the machine, no third party to trust, breach, or leak.

nodes.label: Rule 1: never write to stdout except via the MCP SDK
nodes.rationale: The stdio transport uses stdout as the JSON-RPC channel; any stray print() corrupts the protocol and breaks the session. All logging goes to stderr or a file, for the server and anything it spawns.
nodes.norm_label: rule 1: never write to stdout except via the mcp sdk

nodes.label: Rule 2: always run unprivileged
nodes.rationale: No feature may require sudo or real root; isolation is achieved via a user namespace mapping the unprivileged UID to root inside. If a capability needs real root, it doesn't ship.

nodes.label: Rule 4: tool handlers return errors as content, never raise
nodes.rationale: An unhandled exception can kill the MCP session; every handler path returns readable TextContent including 'Blocked:' messages because the model reads them and adapts.
nodes.norm_label: rule 4: tool handlers return errors as content, never raise

nodes.label: Rule 5: report isolation honestly, every time
nodes.rationale: Every run_code result states what actually protected the run (native/container/namespaces-only/none). Never claim protection that wasn't applied; this is namespace isolation sharing the host kernel, not a hardened VM.
nodes.norm_label: rule 5: report isolation honestly, every time

nodes.label: Rule 6: run everywhere, degrade loudly
nodes.rationale: Native isolation is Linux-only; on macOS/Windows fall back to the container backend. A user who can't install/run it is a lost user. Degrade loudly and clearly, never fail silently.

nodes.label: MCP server (front desk: decides whether/what)
nodes.rationale: Owns protocol, tool schemas, validation/policy, warden invocation with an outer deadline, result shaping, audit logging, config. Never sets up namespaces, touches cgroups, or runs untrusted code in its own process.

nodes.label: Warden (isolation core: decides how)
nodes.rationale: 16-stage lifecycle: become root in a private world (userns), seal every dimension (namespaces, pivot_root, cgroups, seccomp), drop in, run the guest. Order matters: several operations are irreversible or must happen before a privilege boundary closes.

nodes.label: User-namespace root mapping (unshare CLONE_NEWUSER)
nodes.rationale: The key trick for unprivileged operation: the unprivileged real UID maps to UID 0 inside the namespace — full capabilities inside, zero outside; same mechanism as rootless Podman/bubblewrap. Footgun: write deny to setgroups before gid_map or the write fails EPERM.

nodes.label: Empty network namespace (no egress guarantee)
nodes.rationale: CLONE_NEWNET with no veth pair gives the guest only a down loopback interface. The no-network-egress guarantee is free — never bridge it to the host — and is stronger and simpler than firewall rules.

nodes.label: pivot_root filesystem jail
nodes.rationale: Guest sees only a read-only minimal rootfs, a size-capped tmpfs /tmp, and the single bind-mounted workspace. ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, .env are simply not present in the guest filesystem — exfiltration is structurally prevented, not policy-blocked.

nodes.label: cgroups v2 resource cage
nodes.rationale: memory.max OOMs the guest not the host; pids.max contains fork bombs; cpu.max quotas CPU. Just filesystem writes, no ctypes. Unprivileged control needs v2 delegation — degrade gracefully and report it in isolation, don't hard-fail.

nodes.label: seccomp-bpf syscall allowlist
nodes.rationale: Defense-in-depth on top of namespaces + cgroups, kept optional/degradable via pyseccomp — never hand-assemble BPF. Ordering: no_new_privs before seccomp; seccomp last, right before execve, so setup code isn't caught by the filter.

nodes.label: Two nested timeouts (inner authoritative, outer backstop)
nodes.rationale: Inner timeout: the warden kills the guest (authoritative). Outer: asyncio.wait_for protects the server from a wedged warden. Wall-clock enforcement is separate from CPU limits because cpu caps alone don't catch sleep(99999).
nodes.norm_label: two nested timeouts (inner authoritative, outer backstop)

nodes.label: Tool schemas as UX for the model
nodes.rationale: The user of the schema is an LLM reading description fields. Descriptions are prompts; enums/bounds make invalid states unrepresentable; failures must be legible content ('Blocked: ...') so the model reads them and adapts.
nodes.norm_label: tool schemas as ux for the model

nodes.label: Honest scope: kernel-sharing isolation, not a VM boundary
nodes.rationale: Isolation is comparable in mechanism to rootless containers but shares the host kernel, so a kernel privilege-escalation exploit could escape. Goal: make common, realistic agent risks structurally impossible; stating limits honestly is a trust-builder. Never claim 'unescapable'.
nodes.norm_label: honest scope: kernel-sharing isolation, not a vm boundary

nodes.label: M0: skeleton that runs (unsafely)
nodes.rationale: Prove end-to-end plumbing with zero isolation first; a running feedback loop with a live agent makes everything after concrete.

nodes.label: M1: container backend + cross-platform floor
nodes.rationale: Ships before the native warden because it makes Vestibule usable by ~80% of the audience (Windows/macOS with Docker) on day one.

nodes.label: M2: native Linux isolation core (the differentiator)
nodes.rationale: Python + ctypes warden: namespaces, pivot_root, cgroups v2, no_new_privs + optional seccomp. Benchmark cold-start latency vs the container backend — expected 10-50x win is a README headline.
nodes.norm_label: m2: native linux isolation core (the differentiator)

nodes.label: M4: adoption polish + SECURITY.md threat model
nodes.rationale: Ruthless first-run test: fresh machine to working in under 2 minutes or people bounce. Honest threat model ships here.
nodes.norm_label: m4: adoption polish + security.md threat model

nodes.label: Cross-platform strategy: run everywhere, degrade loudly
nodes.rationale: Best-in-class isolation is Linux-only; the tool must still run on macOS (container) and Windows (WSL2 or Docker Desktop). Report isolation honestly per-platform; never fail silently. Non-negotiable for adoption.
nodes.norm_label: cross-platform strategy: run everywhere, degrade loudly

nodes.label: Guest stdin always DEVNULL rule
nodes.rationale: Inheriting the server's stdin would leak the MCP JSON-RPC channel to untrusted code and hangs the child on Windows; established in M0 and applied to all backends.

nodes.label: M1 contract (ContainerBackend + read_workspace)
nodes.rationale: Binding M1 contract written after the Codex adversarial review; incorporates all 20 findings: exact container profile, run lifecycle, capability probing, read_workspace jail, 14 acceptance criteria, 7-step implementation order.

nodes.label: D1: writable dedicated workspace
nodes.rationale: ~/.vestibule/workspace mounted read-write at /workspace: a declared, contained blast radius inside a Vestibule-owned folder, never user data. Rejected read-only+scratch (kills the persistent file output channel) and a per-call workspace_write param (grows the tool surface).

nodes.label: D2: pinned official images, no auto-pull
nodes.rationale: python:3.12-slim (also runs bash) and node:22-slim pinned by digest so image bytes can never silently change. Never pull inside a tool call (multi-minute stall + CLI chatter risk); a missing image returns the exact docker pull command. Rejected custom published image (owning image CI + supply chain too early) and auto-pull at startup (MCP handshake timeout risk).
nodes.norm_label: d2: pinned official images, no auto-pull

nodes.label: D3: tiered degradation (hard controls block, soft limits degrade)
nodes.rationale: Hard controls change what hostile code can reach (network none, cap-drop, no-new-privileges, read-only rootfs, non-root user, workspace mount) — any missing means the run is refused. Soft limits only bound consumption (memory/cpu/pids/tmpfs), all backstopped by the external timeout — a missing one degrades loudly as container-degraded. Plain 'container' only when the full profile verifiably applied.
nodes.norm_label: d3: tiered degradation (hard controls block, soft limits degrade)

nodes.label: D4: Docker-first, runtime-agnostic
nodes.rationale: The backend never assumes a runtime by name — it verifies capabilities via probes, so any runtime that passes is safe. VESTIBULE_RUNTIME=auto|docker|podman from day one; only Docker tested in M1, Podman documented as experimental.

nodes.label: D9: read-only /sandbox script delivery
nodes.rationale: Script written to a per-run host temp dir mounted read-only at /sandbox. Not argv (256 KB code blows Windows command-line limits), not stdin (-i is forbidden), not the workspace (would pollute the persistent channel).

nodes.label: Container execution profile (contract section 3)
nodes.rationale: Exact flag set: network none, cap-drop ALL, no-new-privileges, read-only rootfs, non-root user, mem=swap, cpus, pids-limit, capped tmpfs, no host env inheritance, --rm --init, --pull never, deadline/owner labels. Never present: --privileged, devices, host namespaces, socket mounts, -i/-t.

nodes.label: read_workspace path jail
nodes.rationale: Guest-planted symlinks are the headline attack. Lexical rejection (.. , absolute paths, any colon/ADS, UNC/device prefixes, NUL, reserved device names, trailing dots/spaces) before touching the filesystem; then a component walk refusing symlinks/reparse points, commonpath containment, O_NOFOLLOW opens. Residual TOCTOU documented, not silently ignored.

nodes.label: Backend selection & capability probing (contract section 5)
nodes.rationale: Lazy, once per process, on first tool call so the MCP handshake is never delayed. Full-profile probe is a real run; on failure retry without soft flags (container-degraded); else every run_code is Blocked with the runtime's own error. Misconfiguration is never silent.

nodes.label: S4-D1: bounded semaphore wait, then legible refusal
nodes.rationale: Literal queueing would burn the outer deadline and return a generic message the model cannot adapt to. Instead wait 5 s for a slot then raise RunRefusedError rendered as 'Blocked: too many concurrent runs'. An exception, not a RunResult, because nothing ran — fabricating exit codes for a non-run would be dishonest.
nodes.norm_label: s4-d1: bounded semaphore wait, then legible refusal

nodes.label: S4-D2: honest isolation:none when the runtime binary is missing
nodes.rationale: Step 3's spawn-failure path returned isolation: container for a run that never executed anything. Fix: isolation: none with detail 'runtime unavailable; nothing was executed' — keeps golden rule 5 unbroken. Only fires when the docker binary is gone, not a dead daemon.
nodes.norm_label: s4-d2: honest isolation:none when the runtime binary is missing

nodes.label: S4-D3: deadline-label orphan reaping
nodes.rationale: Replaces the v1 age heuristic after a Codex adversarial review showed a reaper using local max_timeout_s can kill another server's legitimate longer run. Owner stamps vestibule.deadline=<epoch> at spawn; any reaper removes only past-deadline (+60 s margin) labeled containers, never on missing/garbled labels. Also fixes the created-state race and Docker Desktop VM clock drift (no daemon timestamps consulted). Residual risk accepted: multi-host remote daemon with >60 s clock skew.

nodes.label: Detached-task cleanup (Codex P2 fix)
nodes.rationale: Awaiting cleanup on the guest-timeout path with a slow daemon could push the backend past the server's outer deadline, replacing the honest timed-out result with a generic failure. As built: the result returns immediately; the detached finisher task kills/removes the container and owns the concurrency slot until the container is really gone, so max_concurrent bounds existing containers. A CLI wedging after the guest finished is reported as a runtime failure, never a fake timeout.

nodes.label: S5-D1: failed selection cached only 30 s
nodes.rationale: The #1 real failure is Docker Desktop not running; forever-caching failure would force an MCP session restart after fixing it. Success caches for the process lifetime; failure only 30 s, so 'start Docker Desktop, then retry' works in-session. Bonus: cold-VM probe failures self-heal.
nodes.norm_label: s5-d1: failed selection cached only 30 s

nodes.label: S5-D2: degraded retry drops all four soft limits at once
nodes.rationale: When the full profile fails but hard isolation works, one retry runs with memory/cpu/pids/tmpfs-size off and reports exactly that list. Per-limit add-back rejected: up to 4 extra probe containers on the first call could exceed a client's tool-call timeout; revisit only if real degraded environments show up.
nodes.norm_label: s5-d2: degraded retry drops all four soft limits at once

nodes.label: S5-D3: per-run image preflight + --pull never
nodes.rationale: Revised after the Codex review flagged that docker run auto-pulls a missing image by default — a first node run would start a multi-minute network pull inside a tool call, violating D2. Every run preflights its own image (cached image inspect); --pull never is the backstop for the delete-between-check-and-run race. Docker CLI >= 20.10 becomes the documented floor.

nodes.label: Exit-125 honesty rule (dead daemon after a good probe)
nodes.rationale: A dead daemon with the binary still present makes docker run exit 125 with the container never started — S4-D2 does not cover it, and it would have claimed isolation: container for a non-run. Exit 125 reports isolation: none ('nothing was executed') and drops the cached selection so the next call re-probes. A guest deliberately exiting 125 gets underclaimed isolation plus one cheap re-probe — underclaiming is the safe side of golden rule 5.
nodes.norm_label: exit-125 honesty rule (dead daemon after a good probe)

nodes.label: CRLF guest-script corruption bug
nodes.rationale:
Step 3 wrote guest scripts in text mode, turning 
 into 
 on Windows hosts; CRLF breaks bash keywords in the Linux guest (then is not then). Latent since step 3 — Python/Node tolerate CRLF and single-line bash never hit it; the read-only-mode probe was the first multi-line if/then bash guest and failed instantly. Fixed with newline="
" on the script write.

nodes.label: Failed-selection task leak / drain() fix
nodes.rationale: A selection that rejects its probe candidates left their detached cleanup/reaper tasks running; event-loop shutdown mass-cancels them, and a task cancelled mid-create_subprocess_exec can orphan the spawn waiter on Windows (CPython proactor wart), deadlocking loop close — the test suite hung 42 minutes in teardown, diagnosed with py-spy + a pending-task watchdog. Fixed with a bounded ContainerBackend.drain() called on every rejected candidate.

nodes.label: S6-D1: pin the manifest-list digest, proven through the real code path
nodes.rationale: Pin the platform-independent manifest-list digest captured from a fresh local pull (works on x86 and ARM; platform-specific digest would break ARM Macs). Rejected: keeping drifting tags (reopens the supply-chain hole) and lockfiles/remote pin fetch (machinery without payoff for two strings). Capture is not done until the exact refs are proven through the real preflight + --pull never run path. Trade-off stated honestly: pinned bytes get no upstream security fixes until a reviewed re-pin.
nodes.norm_label: s6-d1: pin the manifest-list digest, proven through the real code path

nodes.label: S6-D2: unpinned image overrides warn loudly, never refuse
nodes.rationale: Overriding images is a legitimate power feature (custom toolchains, air-gapped mirrors) — refusal would break real workflows; silence would violate 'misconfiguration is never silent'. One stderr warning per selection pass, covering both configured refs since selection only probes the python image.
nodes.norm_label: s6-d2: unpinned image overrides warn loudly, never refuse

nodes.label: S6-D3: no setup/doctor CLI in M1 — the first tool call is the doctor
nodes.rationale: A standalone checker would duplicate the report surface and add a maintenance promise; the MCP-first path already self-diagnoses — every failure returns the exact fix, and the S5-D1 cooldown makes fix-then-retry work in-session. Deferred as an M4 candidate.
nodes.norm_label: s6-d3: no setup/doctor cli in m1 — the first tool call is the doctor

nodes.label: S6-D4: proof gate runs under both Docker Desktop image stores
nodes.rationale: Proving pins only on whichever store mode this machine is in leaves the other mainstream mode (classic vs containerd) claimed but untested. The capture-day gate runs twice, once per store mode; ~15 minutes of manual toggling settles empirically what the draft settled by argument. If either mode fails, explicit support narrowing comes back as a fresh decision — it does not ship on hope.
nodes.norm_label: s6-d4: proof gate runs under both docker desktop image stores

nodes.label: Tag-drift trap: pull-by-tag no longer satisfies a digest preflight
nodes.rationale: Once the default is image@sha256:X, a plain docker pull of the tag can fetch digest Y after Docker Hub re-points the tag — the image looks present but preflight still fails, and a user following a stale instruction loops forever. Mitigation: every pull instruction, in Blocked messages and docs, uses the full digest-pinned reference verbatim — validated, not trusted, via the S6-D1/S6-D4 gate.
nodes.norm_label: tag-drift trap: pull-by-tag no longer satisfies a digest preflight

nodes.label: M1 design Codex adversarial review (20 ranked findings)
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: Verdict: sound to implement with amendments, no structural rework — but the written design was underspecified enough to pass acceptance criteria while leaving disk exhaustion, orphaned containers, symlink jail escapes, Docker Desktop permission failures, CLI stdio corruption, and unenforced-but-claimed limits unresolved. Demanded a sharper contract.
nodes.norm_label: m1 design codex adversarial review (20 ranked findings)

nodes.label: Finding 4: killing the CLI is not killing the container
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: On inner timeout, killing only the local docker CLI leaves the container running detached in the daemon while the handler reports a timeout. Use named containers; docker kill then rm -f under a separate cleanup deadline; --rm is a convenience, not the cleanup mechanism.
nodes.norm_label: finding 4: killing the cli is not killing the container

nodes.label: Finding 8: symlink jail escape via guest-planted links
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: Guest code can plant a symlink/reparse point in the workspace pointing at host secrets; a naive resolve+prefix check on a later read_workspace call can be bypassed or race. Accept only relative paths, refuse symlinks anywhere in the chain, verify containment with path APIs not string prefix, document residual TOCTOU.
nodes.norm_label: finding 8: symlink jail escape via guest-planted links

nodes.label: Finding 11: unspecified image strategy
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: Without a pinned image strategy, :latest images behave differently next month or fail offline. Pin by digest for repeatability; preflight availability; never auto-pull during run_code.

nodes.label: Finding 13: isolation field too coarse for degraded container behavior
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: Returning isolation: container while memory limits were silently ignored overclaims protection. Keep plain 'container' only for the full approved profile; add container-degraded and per-control detail.
nodes.norm_label: finding 13: isolation field too coarse for degraded container behavior

nodes.label: Finding 19: output volume must be capped during collection, not at formatting
nodes.author: Codex (GPT-5.4)
nodes.rationale: A guest writing 10 GB to stdout harms the host before post-hoc truncation runs. Stream-read with byte caps and kill the container once caps are exceeded.
nodes.norm_label: finding 19: output volume must be capped during collection, not at formatting

nodes.label: Step-6 Codex adversarial review (pass 2)
nodes.author: Codex
nodes.rationale: Verdict: needs-attention. No-ship: the plan relied on a one-mode local validation for a store-specific digest risk, and the code diff regressed honest isolation reporting.

nodes.label: High finding: digest proof gate only validates the current image-store mode
nodes.author: Codex
nodes.rationale: Docker Desktop classic vs containerd image stores can resolve manifest-list refs differently; validating only the mode this machine happens to be in leaves the other supported mode claimed but untested — users there could follow the exact documented command and still fail preflight.
nodes.norm_label: high finding: digest proof gate only validates the current image-store mode

nodes.label: Medium finding: naive.py spawn failure reports isolation="false"
nodes.author: Codex
nodes.rationale: A working-tree edit made NaiveBackend return isolation="false" on FileNotFoundError — not a valid enum value, and selection/server logic keys safety behavior off exact isolation strings. Fix: restore isolation="none" and add a regression test.
nodes.norm_label: medium finding: naive.py spawn failure reports isolation="false"

hyperedges.label: Honest isolation reporting enforcement across milestones
